stem: Why can echo-planar imaging acquire an image extremely rapidly?

options.text: Rapid gradient reversals traverse many or all k-space lines after one excitation
Every k-space point is assumed to have the same value
T1 and T2 relaxation are suspended during acquisition
A separate 90-degree pulse is used for every image pixel

explanation: EPI uses a train of gradient echoes and blipped phase encoding to cover k-space with very few RF excitations. Single-shot EPI can collect the entire matrix in one echo train.

stem: Which acquisition commonly relies on echo-planar readout because temporal speed is critical?

options.text: A conventional multiminute spin-echo localizer only
Diffusion-weighted imaging
Static-field shimming without image acquisition
A proton-density scan requiring no gradients

explanation: DWI commonly uses single-shot EPI to freeze motion and rapidly collect diffusion-sensitive data. Functional and perfusion MRI also benefit from EPI's temporal resolution.

stem: Which artifact combination is characteristic of echo-planar imaging?

options.text: Complete elimination of off-resonance distortion
Only T1 saturation with no geometric effects
Susceptibility distortion and phase ghosting
Loss of all low-spatial-frequency information

explanation: The long, rapidly sampled EPI echo train is highly sensitive to field offsets, eddy currents, and timing errors. These effects produce geometric distortion, signal loss, and ghosting.

stem: What does each echo in a fast spin-echo train usually contribute?

options.text: An identical copy of the same image pixel
A new main magnetic field strength
A separate slice with no spatial encoding
A differently phase-encoded k-space line

explanation: Changing phase encoding between refocused echoes lets one excitation populate multiple k-space lines. Echo train length is therefore a major determinant of acceleration.

stem: Compared with single-shot EPI, what is a typical advantage of fast spin echo?

options.text: Less susceptibility-related geometric distortion
Shorter acquisition than every EPI sequence
No RF energy deposition from refocusing pulses
Complete immunity to motion and blurring

explanation: FSE's 180-degree pulse train refocuses much static inhomogeneity and generally produces less distortion than EPI. It is slower and can have high SAR and echo-train blurring.

stem: What can result from a very long fast spin-echo train?

options.text: Elimination of T2 effects from all echoes
Image blurring as different k-space lines experience different T2 decay
A mandatory increase in chemical shift frequency
Perfect recovery of signal lost to molecular interactions

explanation: Signal amplitude changes across the echo train because of T2 decay, so k-space is weighted unevenly. Long trains accelerate imaging but can soften edges and increase RF power deposition.
